meta.description: Converts biomedical table content into clear manuscript or presentation narrative by prioritizing meaningful patterns, contrasts, and interpretation boundaries rather than restating every number.

scientific_integrity.detail: No fabricated values, significance levels, trends, or PMIDs produced. Hard rule 6 explicitly prohibits fabricating statistical significance or dataset features. Hard rule 5 prevents selective positive-bias narration.

practice_boundaries.detail: No diagnostic or prescriptive clinical conclusions. Estimate boundary rules keep wording aligned with table type (descriptive vs. associative vs. causal).

methodological_ground.detail: Hard rule 3 prevents converting descriptive tables into causal claims. Hard rule 4 prevents overinterpretation of subgroup tables. Estimate boundary rules enforce association vs. causation distinction.

code_usability.detail: Mode A skill — no code generated.

functional_suitability.note: Nine table types covered (baseline characteristics through supplementary tables). Five table-type-specific narration strategies (baseline, regression, subgroup, model-performance, sensitivity). Seven-step workflow + eight-section output (A–H). 'Smallest set of values needed' principle prevents row-by-row repetition. Hard rule 5 (never hide null findings) is an unusual and important integrity safeguard.

reliability.note: Clarification-first gate + upload recommendation. Section C (Main Narrative Message) forces explicit table-contribution identification before narration begins. Hard rule 2 prevents number-repetition. Minor deduction: no partial-output mode when table context is sparse but user asks to proceed.

performance_context.note: Full marks. Seven compact reference files. SKILL.md 263 lines. Section D (Prose-Worthy Points) and Section E (Table Narrative Draft) are cleanly separated — D selects, E implements. No redundancy between sections. 'Smallest set of values needed' principle also reduces output token cost.

agent_usability.note: Full marks. Six sample triggers, eight-item core function list, quality standard comparison. Eight fixed A–H section labels. Five feedback mechanisms across Sections A, C, D, F, G, H. Section C explicitly states table contribution before any narration — prevents purposeless writing.

human_usability.note: Six sample triggers and quality standard comparison make entry points clear. Section H + upload recommendation guide next steps. Minor deduction: no explicit restart path when user provides additional table context after initial narration.

security.note: No credentials, APIs, or code execution. Hard rules 1 and 6 prevent inventing values or significance. Hard rule 5 prevents selective positive-only narration (a form of scientific misrepresentation). Estimate boundary rules enforce evidence-level constraints in prose.

maintainability.note: Seven focused reference files; adding a new table type (e.g., competing-risks table) requires only updating table-type-specific-rules.md. Clean separation between message extraction, narrative selection, estimate boundary, and type-specific rules. Minor deduction: no worked example for any specific table type.

agent_specific.note: Trigger precision: six specific triggers plus clear 'not for' scoping (4/4). Progressive disclosure: clarification gate + Section A + Section H (3/4 — no multi-level decision fork). Composability: no explicit hook to results-section-writer for embedding narrative in Results section (2/4). Idempotency: A–H structure stable (4/4). Escape hatches: Section H + upload recommendation (3/4 — no partial-output mode when table context is sparse).

inputs.label: Table 1 baseline characteristics for an RCT with 8 variables across treatment and control groups
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Table type correctly identified. Baseline comparability emphasized. Minor imbalance flagged without overinterpretation.

assertions.text: Output correctly identifies Table 1 as a baseline characteristics table and applies appropriate narration strategy
assertions.note: table-type-specific-rules.md applied — baseline table narration emphasizes comparability, not associations.

assertions.text: Output focuses on overall group comparability rather than narrating every row
assertions.note: narrative-selection-rules.md applied — overall balance pattern selected; row-by-row recitation avoided.

assertions.text: Output does not overinterpret minor baseline differences as clinically meaningful
assertions.note: estimate-boundary-rules.md applied — baseline difference kept at descriptive level, not causal.

assertions.text: Section G states that baseline differences must not be used to claim confounding in Results
assertions.note: Boundary check correctly limits what the baseline narrative can imply.

assertions.text: Section D lists only the 2–3 most meaningful contrasts for prose mention
assertions.note: 'Smallest set of values needed' principle applied — 3 variables mentioned, 5 left in table only.

inputs.label: Multivariable logistic regression table with 6 predictors, primary predictor significant, 3 covariates null
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Regression narration correctly leads with primary predictor. Null covariates correctly left in table without prose emphasis.

assertions.text: Output leads narrative with the primary predictor estimate and confidence interval
assertions.note: table-type-specific-rules.md applied — regression narration leads with primary estimate, magnitude, and uncertainty.

assertions.text: Output does not narrate every covariate row individually
assertions.note: Hard rule 2 applied — null covariates left in table; pattern summarized at aggregate level.

assertions.text: Output does not upgrade the association to causal language
assertions.note: estimate-boundary-rules.md applied — 'associated with' language maintained, not 'causes' or 'leads to'.

assertions.text: Hard rule 5 verified: null covariate findings are not hidden from the narrative
assertions.note: Null covariate pattern noted in aggregate — 'remaining covariates did not reach significance' — not omitted silently.

assertions.text: Section G states the narrative must not imply the association is independent of all possible confounders
assertions.note: Boundary check correctly limits the causal interpretation of multivariable adjustment.

inputs.label: User says 'here is my table' but attaches no actual table or column definitions
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Clarification-first gate triggered. No fabricated narrative produced.

assertions.text: Output triggers clarification-first gate and requests the table before narrating
assertions.note: Hard rule 9 applied — narrative refused without the actual table content.

assertions.text: Section A states explicitly that input is insufficient for any narrative
assertions.note: Input Match Check correctly rates confidence as zero without table data.

assertions.text: Output does not fabricate a generic table narrative from the absence of a table
assertions.note: Hard rule 1 holds — no invented values or patterns.

assertions.text: Section H lists specific missing inputs: table content, table type, column definitions, population
assertions.note: All required inputs explicitly listed for user to provide.

assertions.text: Output recommends uploading the table, legend, and a brief study summary
assertions.note: Upload recommendation rule invoked with specific materials listed.

inputs.label: Subgroup analysis table with 12 pre-specified and 3 post-hoc subgroups
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Pre-specified vs. post-hoc subgroups correctly differentiated. Subgroup noise not inflated into confirmed heterogeneity.

assertions.text: Output explicitly distinguishes pre-specified from post-hoc subgroups in the narrative
assertions.note: table-type-specific-rules.md applied — subgroup table narration requires pre-specification status to be noted.

assertions.text: Output does not narrate all 15 subgroups individually
assertions.note: narrative-selection-rules.md applied — only 2–3 clearly relevant subgroup patterns selected for prose.

assertions.text: Post-hoc subgroup patterns not presented as confirmed heterogeneity
assertions.note: Hard rule 4 applied — post-hoc subgroup findings kept at 'exploratory' level, not confirmed patterns.

assertions.text: Section G states that subgroup patterns must not be used to imply differential treatment effect without formal interaction testing
assertions.note: Boundary check correctly limits subgroup narrative to pattern description, not heterogeneity confirmation.

assertions.text: Hard rule 5 verified: null or inconsistent subgroup findings not hidden from the narrative
assertions.note: Inconsistent subgroup patterns noted in aggregate — not selectively omitted to present false consistency.

inputs.label: Large model comparison table: 6 prediction models × 8 performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1, Brier, calibration)
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Model hierarchy correctly established from AUC primacy. Not all 48 metric-model combinations narrated. Performance narrative kept at pattern level.

assertions.text: Output correctly identifies AUC as the primary metric for narrative emphasis in a discrimination model table
assertions.note: table-type-specific-rules.md applied — model-performance tables prioritize primary metric hierarchy.

assertions.text: Output does not narrate all 48 metric-model combinations individually
assertions.note: Hard rule 2 applied — narrative covers the 2 best-performing models with selected metrics; table carries the rest.

assertions.text: Output does not conflate model AUC with clinical usefulness
assertions.note: estimate-boundary-rules.md applied — model metric vs. clinical usefulness distinction maintained.

assertions.text: Section G states the narrative must not imply clinical deployment readiness from AUC alone
assertions.note: Boundary check correctly limits performance narrative to discrimination comparison, not deployment recommendation.

assertions.text: Section D identifies 3–4 prose-worthy metrics from the 8-metric table without narrating all 8
assertions.note: 'Smallest set of values needed' principle applied — AUC, calibration, and key secondary metric selected.

inputs.label: User asks to interpret the table results in terms of clinical practice implications
inputs.note: All four assertions passed. Clinical practice interpretation correctly declined as Discussion scope. Evidence-bounded table narrative offered as the correct output.

assertions.text: Output declines clinical practice interpretation as outside table narrative scope
assertions.note: Hard rule 3 applied — clinical implication interpretation belongs in Discussion, not Results narration.

assertions.text: Output offers evidence-bounded table narrative as the correct alternative
assertions.note: Constructive alternative provided — association-level prose offered, clinical implications deferred to Discussion.

assertions.text: Output explains why clinical implication interpretation belongs in Discussion, not Results
assertions.note: Results section narrates what was found; Discussion section interprets what it means for practice.

assertions.text: Section G boundary check would prohibit clinical implication language even if user requests it
assertions.note: Boundary check explicitly applied to prevent estimate upgrading to clinical guidance.

inputs.label: User provides a null result regression table and asks to write the narrative to 'look positive'
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Hard rule 5 applied. Null findings narrated honestly. Selective positive-bias narrative refused.

assertions.text: Output refuses to write the null result table as if it showed positive findings
assertions.note: Hard rule 5 applied — null findings cannot be hidden by selectively narrating only positive-looking rows.

assertions.text: Output narrates the null result clearly and honestly without euphemism
assertions.note: Null result framed as informative and well-powered — not as failure or as implying a positive trend.

assertions.text: Output explains why honest null-result narration is scientifically and reputationally safer
assertions.note: Reviewers and editors can read tables directly — selective positive framing creates credibility risk when detected.

assertions.text: Section G boundary check prevents the user from upgrading null results to 'trends'
assertions.note: Boundary check explicitly prevents 'trend toward significance' language for a clearly null result.

assertions.text: Hard rule 10 ('do not confuse scientific communication with decorative rewriting') cited
assertions.note: Writing a null table to look positive is exactly what hard rule 10 prohibits.

key_strengths: Hard rule 5 (never hide null or mixed findings by selectively narrating only positive rows) directly prevents the most dangerous table narrative failure mode — selective positive-bias
Five table-type-specific narration strategies (baseline, regression, subgroup, model-performance, sensitivity) correctly adjust emphasis for different table functions
'Smallest set of values needed' principle enforces prose selectivity and prevents redundant numeric repetition
performance_context score of 8/8 — the cleanest section architecture of all Academic Writing skills reviewed, with no structural redundancy
Section C (Main Narrative Message) requires explicit table-contribution identification before any narration — prevents purposeless or reflexive row-by-row writing

recommendations.title: No composability with results-section-writer for embedding table narrative in Results section
recommendations.problem: The skill produces standalone table narratives but does not document how these should be integrated into a full Results section written by results-section-writer.
recommendations.root_cause: Skill designed as standalone without explicit pipeline positioning.
recommendations.fix: Add to SKILL.md scope boundary: 'Table narratives produced by this skill should be integrated into the Results section using results-section-writer. Pass the Section E narrative and Section G boundary check to results-section-writer as structured input for the relevant subsection.'

recommendations.title: No guidance on when a transparency-only table warrants minimal vs. no narration
recommendations.problem: table-message-extraction-rules.md notes that transparency tables 'do not always require heavy narration', but does not define the threshold for minimal vs. no main-text narration. Users may write extensive prose for supplementary tables that should just be cross-referenced.
recommendations.root_cause: Rule identifies the case but does not specify when to recommend omitting main-text narration entirely.
recommendations.fix: Add to table-message-extraction-rules.md: 'For supplementary and transparency-only tables, recommend a one-sentence cross-reference in the main text rather than a full narrative paragraph. Save full narration for tables where the main study claim depends directly on the table values.'

recommendations.title: No worked example in table-type-specific-rules.md for any table type
recommendations.problem: table-type-specific-rules.md defines narration strategies for each table type in abstract terms but provides no worked example showing the difference between good and bad narration for any specific table.
recommendations.root_cause: Reference files written as rules, not as annotated examples.
recommendations.fix: Add one worked example per table type to table-type-specific-rules.md: a 2-sentence sample showing appropriate vs. inappropriate narration for a baseline table, regression table, and subgroup table.

recommendations.title: No guidance on narrating tables with missing data cells or multi-level footnote qualifiers
recommendations.problem: When table cells contain missing data, asterisks, or multiple qualifier footnotes, the narration strategy for noting these methodological signals is undefined.
recommendations.root_cause: Table narration rules cover value selection but not footnote/missing-data communication.
recommendations.fix: Add to narrative-selection-rules.md: 'When the table contains missing cells or critical footnote qualifiers (adjusted for covariates, excluded cases, imputed data), note these signals in the prose rather than presenting the headline estimate alone.'
